speaker: interviewee
text: This is only the first tape?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 2, Aaron Elster.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. When we were herded out of the hiding place, my mother was separated from us. My dad, myself, and my little sister, and other people were against the wall outside the building, being guarded by Ukrainians. They were extremely mean, extremely mean, extremely vicious.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were kicking and bayoneting people, and shooting people who didn't move fast enough. There was no particular reason. It was just at will. And I was frightened. But what I noticed is my mother was not there anymore. My mother was separated from us. And it was just my dad and I and all kinds of other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your little sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Standing next to me. And I was shaking. And I couldn't stop shaking. And I was crying and pleading and crying, and I knew what was happening. And I was very fearful of death. I didn't want to die. And when they finally got all the people out of the hiding place, they marched us up to the marketplace. The marketplace was a gathering place where on Thursdays, the farmers would come and sell their wares and people would exchange. And they set us around a square in the marketplace. In the middle was a German soldier, a German SS, with a machine gun. And to the side were guards marching around. And I was sitting with my dad. We were all made to sit on the floor-- on the ground. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I just couldn't control myself. There was such a tremendous fear of death, not so much dying, but the pain of dying that was instilled in me because I saw my uncle get shot and what he went through. One of my uncles got shot. He jumped off a train and he got shot through here and here and the sores festered. And the pain that he was in, that was in my mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was saying to my dad, you know, what should he do? What should I do? And he-- and he said that I should run. But before I get to that, my mind was full of fear, hate, questioning. Why is God allowing this? What did we do, or what did I do to deserve this? And I would almost be willing to make a deal with God that if the Earth would open up and we would all be consumed, I would be much happier than just us. In other words, if the Germans would die with us, it would make me happier.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my thinking. That was my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wanted to live. It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad decided--- said, why don't you run? You'll run to the farm where we came from. What I did is, is I backed up to the back of where the people were sitting, and I got on my stomach and I crawled about 10 yards, 15 yards. And at the end of the marketplace, there was like an open sewer. You know, in the old country, there was no sewers. When it rained, the water used to run down there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I crawled in that sewer for about 30, 40 yards, because there was building at the end of the market-- buildings at the end of the marketplace, and just stood up and started running. And for the life of me, I don't understand why nobody saw me and nobody shot-- shot after me. And I ran for about two, three blocks to the end of the street where the ghetto was ending. And I had an uncle that had a house there, so I ran into the house to hide myself, and I'm looking for a place where to hide. And keep in mind, the town was already totally liquidated. We were the last remnants to be liquidated. And there was a carpet on the floor. And I knew there was a basement there, a cellar. So I lifted up the carpet, and lo and behold, there was an opening to go into the cellar. I go into the cellar and I find my little niece, who I have a picture of, and her mother were hiding in that cellar. I was there for the rest of the day and-- and that night. And the next morning, I said to my aunt, we've got to get out of here, because they're going to come back and they're going to find us. And she was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that time, were you wondering about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what I was wondering. I think I was probably-- I-- I don't know. My mind must have been wondering about everything. I was just preoccupied with trying to save myself. There's no way that I wanted to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the next morning, I says that we should run away, and they didn't want to. They were afraid. I just got out of the cellar, went in the back of the house. In back of the house was the wire fences for the ghetto. And I remember I had my-- my sister's coat on and I had a pair of short pants. And there was a Polish woman on the outside of the fence saying [POLISH], in other words, 'Come, Jew.' And I went to the fence, went under the wires, and just started running. And I ran to the farm. I don't even know how I found it, because I was only there once. So there are certain things I cannot explain to you. How did I find that farm? But I found it. And I found my dad there, because he escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My little sister didn't make it, and that's one thing that I've never been able to really resolve in my-- in my heart. I've sort of gotten used to the idea of my parents being dead, and my father and mother and so on. But my little sister was 6 years of age.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a tremendous ache when I think about her last minutes of life, because what I'm wondering is, who held her hand? [CRYING] Who told her that they cared for her? Who consoled her to tell her that it was all right? She was just 6 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I also feel some-- some kind of guilt, because I ran away and left her there. Not that there was anything I could have done, but she died among strangers. There was nobody there to tell her that they loved her, and that's a pain that will never go away. But anyway, I got to the farm and I found my dad there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you when you found your father there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was great happiness, great joy to find a survivor. Now, my sister was already living with that Polish couple, OK? And I knew that. I didn't know where yet, but I knew she was in the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my mother was spared, put on a labor-- a labor battalion in the town, to clean up, to pack all the belongings to be shipped to Germany. We decided to go back to town, because there were-- there were remnants of Jews in the ghetto that they were using for labor. And I don't know why people still believed that they were going to spare certain people, OK? It's something that's-- it's unexplainable. So my dad and I went back to the town and we found our mother there. She was working there. I stayed with my mother and my dad was separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: He stayed someplace else. Why, I don't know. I didn't see my dad after-- after I separated. I didn't see him anymore. I stayed with my mother for probably three, four weeks. And that would bring us into, I think, November of 1942. It was getting cold. And then they came, the SS and their Ukrainian lackeys, to liquidate the balance of the labor force, to liquidate the balance of the Jews. And my mother got wind of that. And her and a friend and myself, we started running just probably minutes before they got there. And we ran into a farm. And as I indicated to you before, my parents knew the surrounding-- the people in the surrounding areas. And that was nighttime. I didn't understand why my-- why this guy was with us, this man. His name was Gedalia. Didn't know what the situation was. I suspected something, but I-- I don't know to this day. But he was with her and I was with them. And we went into a farm. And my mother went inside to negotiate with this Pole. And then she came out and she told me that I have to leave. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She suggested that I go and talk and find Mrs. Gurski. Maybe she will take me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was Mrs. Gurski?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the Polish lady that my sister was staying with. They were an older couple. I'm skipping something. I found out where they were because when-- when I came back to the city, to the town, my mother and I in the evening time would walk by that house. So she would point it out to me, this is where your sister was staying, OK. She loaded her up with clothes and some jewelry and things that she was able to give her. So I knew where she was. But I wasn't sure whether I could make it back into the town, because there was no Jews left there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like when your mother told you to  leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was-- I haven't been able to deal with this for probably 50 years. But I've become to rationalize it why. If we didn't separate, we-- I would have been dead. There might have been some other reasons which I-- I can't explain. All I know is I felt abandoned at that time. I couldn't talk to my sister about this. I kept this inside of me for probably 50 years. I'm able to talk about it now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm trying to rationalize why she did what she did. Was it for my benefit? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was it a matter of survival? Was it for her benefit? I cannot fathom a parent doing this with a child, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943-- I was 10 years old. So whatever the reason was, I didn't accept it well. I tried to make it on my own. She gave me some jewelry that she had on her possession. I remember I had some earrings and some-- some gold rings. And I went to another farm. And I walked up to that farmer's house, and I asked them whether they would take me in. I gave them what I had. They put me down the cellar. And I thought maybe they were going to hide me. Two hours later-- they fed me. They gave me some food. Two hours later they asked me to come up. They said that they were afraid. They couldn't do this, because if the Germans found out, they would kill them and us, and that I've got to be real careful if the Germans find me not to say that they helped me, because that was a death sentence to any Pole that helped a Jew. It was also-- I was also fearful of the fact that the Poles would kill me, because the Poles were killing Jews. And I was afraid to go back into the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did those people give you back what you had given them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were afraid to keep it or they just gave it back to me. I'm not really sure that I blame them much, because of the fear that-- of, you know, you help a Jew, you're dead. But in spite of it, your life is so important that you want somebody to help you. I tried to make it on my own. I used to-- I would sneak into barns during the day and stay there all day. And at night, I would go out and attack the-- they used to bury the beets, sugar beets in the ground over the winter. And I would eat those beets. They were quite good, OK. And it was getting cold and I was getting sick. And I still had my short pants on. And this must have been already close to December. And if I would reveal myself to a Polish couple, I would go in there and beg for food. Some would give me. Some would chase me away. It didn't last too long. I don't think I was on my own for more than maybe two, three weeks. I just-- I was getting too sick. So the choices were to give myself up or to try and see whether this Polish woman would take me in. And that's what I was being encouraged to do, to give myself up, by every Pole that I came in-- that I had an encounter with. Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't-- why didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to die. I just didn't want to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were sure you would if you gave yourself up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there's no question about it. And as I said, you know, the pain, the death, the fear of the pain was tremendous in my mind. So I went back to the ghetto. There was not a soul there that night. The ghetto was totally empty. Walked into a house, just any house, lay down on the floor, I went to sleep. I was so tired. And the next morning I was going to go see the Gurskis. I got up. It must have been about 6:00 in the morning. It was getting light already. And then I had to go from that place all the way where they lived. And I was afraid that people were going to recognize me, see me, or anything like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just walked. Nobody bothered me. Went up to the house. It was like two apartments downstairs. She had a small apartment upstairs. And on the left side was a raw attic, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mr. Gurski was just coming down to go to church, coming down the stairs. And I says-- and I said to him that I came because I know my sister is, here and my mother said that you people would help me. And he called out to his wife to come out, and he just kept going to go to church. And she says [POLISH], which means 'get away from here. Your sister is not here.' And I begged her to please let me stay for a few days. And I continued to do so, and I was crying and I was sick. Apparently I must have gotten to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she told me, OK, I'll let you up in the attic. I'll let you stay there for a couple of days. Then you got to go. Your sister's not here. So I got up in the attic. And I was full of lice. I was full of vermin by that time. And on the attic, there was-- on the side, there was a place where I could lay down, and I don't know, with a blanket. There was something that I had to cover myself. And I stayed there for a couple of days.

speaker: interviewee
text: She brought me in some food. She was very mad at me for coming there, because I was a danger to her. And I was mad at myself for coming to her, because I was putting her in danger. But yet the will to live is so great that you're hoping that these things don't happen. Two, three days later she sent my sister in from the apartment, because my sister stayed in the house with her. She sent her in with a pail of water and some soap to try and clean me up a little bit. I was a very unwelcome party in that place all the time that was there-- that I was there. He never spoke to me once in the year and a half, the close to two years that I was there. She would bring in some food to me once a day. And then I would have a pot to do my stuff and she would take it out. And she would curse at me, and-- that I was a curse to them, and that I was a threat to my sister's survival, too, because my sister was in the apartment so she could hide her better. You can hide a girl better than you can a-- a man. And it was a problem with food. She didn't have that much. She used to do the laundry for the people downstairs, although she owned that building, so she collected rent. There was a schoolteacher downstairs that lived, and there was another couple. And basically, that's where I spent my time, in the attic. Never went outside once. And the cold-- I was extremely cold. I always felt that my fingers and toes were freezing. And the summer was extremely hot, because I was right-- there was a tin roof. And the sun beating down on it was extremely hot. And I used to sit there and cry and pray in my own way, and make deals with God again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was not very grateful. I seemed very rebellious even.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know. I was grateful for them, but I was afraid of them, because they were constantly demeaning me and-- and threatening me and telling me what a terrible thing I did to come there, to-- to cause them that kind of problem. She didn't have the heart, apparently, to chase us out. Plus the fact there's another problem. If she chased me out and if the Germans caught me, and if they beat me, I would probably tell them who hid me. And that-- that was a-- and that's what she threatened me with. That's what she constantly said to us, my sister and I, if the Germans catch you, you're going to say who helped you, and they're going to kill us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a dichotomy of things. She wanted to help us. She wanted to get rid of us. She couldn't get rid of us, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to go crazy there, just sitting around, killing lice. I used to make designs. I used to pee on the floor, you know, walking around. One time he was going down to church. I got carried away and peed right on his head. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That didn't make any friends or influence people, but I remember doing those crazy things. I remember being able to scream and holler and sing or cry when it was raining, because otherwise I couldn't make any noise. When it was raining, the pounding of the rain on the tin roof was so dramatic, it was so loud, that it drowned out anything that I did. Constantly hungry, wondering what would it be like to eat enough of food where you're able to say, I'm full. I never knew what that feeling was like, because she used to bring in something once a day, some soup or something. And sometimes in the evening time, I used to sneak out in the hallway. She had some pigs downstairs. And she would throw out the-- the leftover food into a trough and she would mash it all up. And sometimes they would even pee in it. And I would go out at night and sneak out and steal a handful of that, and I would eat it. And it was a commodity. There was another thing that he had. He had an apple tree in his backyard. And in the morning, he used to have a piece of rye bread-- because my sister used to tell me that-- and an apple, tea, or whatever. And then he'd go to church. He was a retired railroad man.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the fall, he would pick all the apples, put them in the attic. He would put a layer of straw and put all the apples close together and then cover them with straw. And I was constantly hungry. And I couldn't just take an apple, so I had to re-engineer the whole setup, in other words just move the apples just a fraction of an inch apart so I would be able to take an apple out and put everything back together. Finally he got wise to that because after a while-- after a little while, the hole-- the separations became larger. But, you know, when you're hungry, you'll do anything. So I used to have dreams that I was going to be the only child that survived. I don't know why. I pictured myself far away in a country like America, believe this or not. I pictured myself that I was going to be someplace. I was dressed in a shirt and tie. I was on the third floor in an office someplace. I saw myself as an adult.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think things like that kept you going, because you had to have some kind of hope. And once in a while she would come in and tell us the bad news that the Russians were being defeated, that the Germans were advancing, and so on. So that's basically the way life went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you spend much time with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Towards the end. She was in the apartment. I would be by myself in the attic. Towards the end, she was-- she came in, spent some time in the attic. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I-- see, I wasn't part of the deal as far as being hidden, so there's nothing she could do for me. She was afraid to antagonize them, OK. But anyway, my mother, as I said, was in hiding with this man. And about four or five months before the-- before we got freed, before the Russians came in, she came in to tell us that my mother was just brought into town on a wagon, tied to a wagon, and the-- to the German police, and she was shot. So my mother and that guy survived in hiding for about a year, year and a half. And they finally were turned in by this Pole. In fact, my niece started writing to Poland to get the court documents because after the war, those people were taken to-- that Polack, Dzujemblo was his name, was taken to court. And he was sentenced to five year in prison. And I have the documents from Poland, the court documents that we were finally able to get out after-- after 50 years. And there's testimony that I gave and my sister gave in court. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They came to us and the-- the family said that they would kill us if we would testify against him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1945, right after the liberation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I made a statement in here indicating that he helped me and he gave me food when I was in hiding, which was a total lie. And my sister said basically the same thing. I heard subsequently through a visit that my sister made to Israel that this man was killed, this Dzujemblo was killed by a Jewish policeman.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what he did? He found her in a barn and he tied her to a wagon and he tied the other man to a wagon, and he called his neighbors. And they called the police. And the police didn't come fast enough for him, so he dragged them to-- to town in the wagon. My mother had a ring, a wedding ring, and he couldn't get it off, so he chopped her finger off. So you can imagine the-- the agony she went through before she died.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was marched to the cemetery-- this was in the court records-- and she was-- they laid them down on the floor and they shot them. So she survived to almost four months before liberation. My dad, I don't know. I think he wound up in Treblinka, OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in 1945, the Germans started losing the war. The Russians were advancing closer to us. No, that was the end of '44, the end of '44, because we were liberated before the war ended. And the irony of this whole thing, as the front became closer, the Germans chased the tenants out of the two lower apartments, and the German soldiers moved in there. And I was in the attic. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: One guy got drunk one night, and he got up the stairs. And he went into the attic, but I was hiding on the side. And she came running out, what do you want? And she walked him downstairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians started bombing the town, and the bombs were fierce.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was still your hometown area?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in hometown. My whole time was spent in my hometown. And a bomb fell near the building, near the house. And it blew a good portion of the roof off, and I was sitting in the attic. This is-- this is crazy. My sister was with me in the attic. The Germans escaped. They went away now.

speaker: interviewee
text: So bombs falling all over the place, my sister and I run downstairs. And we found food and marmalade. And we're laying on the floor. We're eating bread and marmalade. The bombs are falling. It's like 4th of July outside. And I was having a ball. OK? So that ended. So we thought we were free. But the bombs stopped. The Russians didn't come. We didn't have a place to stay up, because there was no roof left. She had a chicken coop, so she put us in the chicken coop.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Germans were still there. About four or five days later, the Russians came in. And they were looking for food and they were looking for things. They opened up the door and put the gun and my sister is standing there. They don't know what to do, you know. They left us alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now the Russians are in. This is the end of 1944. And the survivors start to reappear into the city. And there was about 20-some-odd survivors. I remember two families that made it in whole, husband and wife, husband and wife and two children. They were hidden by a Polish couple. And some single people appeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were the only children that survived from the whole town, that survived on their own. There were some that came back-- that escaped to Russia that came back to Poland, or some that survived with their parents in hiding, one parent or two parents. Now, she's afraid to let us out, because the Poles may kill us. So after the Russians are in, we're still in that coop for about a week. She finally went to the area where the Jews were congregating. And a woman by the name of Greenberg-- Rafalowich was one family and Greenberg was the other family-- came to the coop to get us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- had you known during that week before that the Russians were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians were there. But she says, you can't go out because the Poles might kill-- will kill you. So anyway, she came, opens up the door, takes a look at us. I can't walk, I'm so weak and so skinny. I must have weighed 50, 60 pounds. My sister is in not much better shape.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they helped us walk to-- to where the Jews were. They were all in one building, living in one building. They congregated, OK? And we basically stayed with them for a little while. And then different episodes started happening.
